title: End the Deep State Act
plain_summary: This law creates a new type of government job for workers who handle secret information and help make important government decisions. These workers can be more easily moved or replaced when a new President comes into office, unlike regular government employees who have job protection. Government agency leaders must look at their current workers and decide which ones should be put into this new job category. The law removes some job protections from these positions but still says workers cannot be treated unfairly.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the End the Deep State Act. The full official name is the Enabling Necessary Discipline with the Defense of Executives' Endeavors to Properly Staff Their Agencies with Trustworthy Employees Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Sense of Congress
sections.text: Congress believes three important things. First, all federal workers must be held responsible for their actions. Second, federal workers get their power from the President, so they must answer to the President. The President is the only person in the executive branch, besides the Vice President, who is elected directly by the American people. Third, the President and the people he appoints must be able to trust the federal workers who handle secret information and help make important government policies.

sections.title: Section 3 Definition
sections.text: In this law, when we say a position is normally subject to change as a result of a Presidential transition, we mean jobs where the workers are expected to quit when a new President takes office. This includes all jobs where the White House Office of Presidential Personnel must approve the appointment.

sections.title: Section 4 Excepted Service
sections.text: Workers will be hired for jobs that handle secret information and help make government policies under a new system called Schedule Policy Career. This applies to these types of jobs when they are not normally expected to change when a new President takes office. These jobs will be part of the excepted service as described in Section 5 of this law.

sections.title: Section 5 Schedule Policy Career of the Excepted Service
sections.text: The Office of Personnel Management (OPM) will organize different types of government jobs into six groups called schedules. Schedule A includes jobs that are not secret or policy making where it is not practical to give tests to job candidates. Schedule B includes jobs that are not secret or policy making where it is not practical to hold competitive tests, but some non competitive testing may be required. Schedule C includes secret or policy making jobs that normally change when a new President takes office. Schedule D includes jobs that are not secret or policy making but where normal hiring rules make it hard to recruit enough students or recent graduates from schools. Schedule E includes administrative law judges who are appointed under existing federal law. Schedule Policy Career includes career jobs that handle secret information and help make policies but are not normally expected to change when a new President takes office. Agencies should give preference to veterans when hiring for Schedule Policy Career jobs when possible. Most civil service rules about firing workers will not apply to jobs in Schedules A, C, D, E, or Policy Career, unless required by law. Civil service firing rules will still apply to Schedule B workers who have competitive status. The Director of OPM must create rules to carry out this law by January nineteen, two thousand twenty nine. The Director must also provide guidance on how to quickly and smoothly switch from current hiring processes to the new Schedule Policy Career process.

sections.title: Section 6 Agency Actions
sections.text: Each head of an executive agency must review their agency's jobs by specific dates. They must do a quick review by April twenty, two thousand twenty five, and a complete review by August eighteen, two thousand twenty five. After that, they must review these jobs at least once every year. The Government Accountability Office is not included in this requirement. After these reviews, agency heads must ask the Director of OPM to recommend that the President put certain competitive service jobs into Schedule Policy Career if they involve secret information or policy making and do not normally change with new Presidents. For jobs that are already excepted from competitive service by law, agency heads must decide which ones involve secret information and policy making and publish this decision in the Federal Register. These requirements apply to both existing jobs and new jobs that are created. When doing these reviews, agency heads should especially consider jobs that involve helping develop or advocate for policies, supervising lawyers, having significant discretion in how the agency operates, working with secret policy documents, conducting union negotiations for the agency, supervising Schedule Policy Career employees, or other duties the Director says may be appropriate. The Director must quickly recommend to the President which jobs should be placed in Schedule Policy Career. Agency heads must also ask the Federal Labor Relations Authority to determine if Schedule Policy Career positions should be excluded from union bargaining units.

sections.title: Section 7 Prohibited Personnel Practices Prohibited
sections.text: Agencies must create rules that prevent unfair treatment of workers or job applicants in Schedule Policy Career positions. These rules must follow the same standards that protect other federal workers from unfair personnel practices. Workers in Schedule Policy Career jobs are not required to personally or politically support the current President or the administration's policies. However, they are required to faithfully carry out administration policies to the best of their ability, consistent with their constitutional oath and the fact that executive power belongs only to the President. If they fail to do this, they can be fired.

sections.title: Section 8 Conforming Regulatory Changes
sections.text: The Director must quickly change the Civil Service Regulations to remove all changes made by a rule from April nine, two thousand twenty four called Upholding Civil Service Protections and Merit System Principles. These changes interfere with the purposes of Executive Order thirteen thousand nine hundred fifty seven. Until these changes are removed, certain specific regulations will not be enforced or have any effect.

sections.title: Section 9 Additional Positions for Consideration
sections.text: Within thirty days after this law is passed, the Director must work with the Executive Office of the President to provide guidance about other types of jobs that executive departments and agencies should consider recommending for Schedule Policy Career.

sections.title: Section 10 Revocation
sections.text: Executive Order fourteen thousand three from January twenty two, two thousand twenty one called Protecting the Federal Workforce will no longer have any power or effect. Any rules, regulations, guidance, or agency policies created under that Executive Order will not be enforced. The heads of executive departments and agencies must review and find existing agency actions related to that Executive Order about discipline and performance policies. They must suspend, change, or cancel those actions as soon as possible.

sections.title: Section 11 General Provisions
sections.text: If any part of this law is found to be invalid by a court, the rest of the law will still be in effect. Nothing in this law should be understood as limiting the positions that can be listed in Schedule C.
